section.title: Answer Me When I Call
section.sectionRationale: An evening companion to Psalm 3's morning cry, a lament that turns to instruct doubters before closing in the Psalter's classic image of peaceful sleep under God's care alone.
section.thesis: David asks God to answer his prayer, calls those who chase empty lies to consider their hearts and be still, and finds a joy and peace in the LORD greater than any harvest, sufficient to let him sleep in safety.
section.frameNote: Three movements marked by Selah: the opening plea and rebuke of the honor-seeking crowd (vv1-2), a call to search the heart and trust the LORD (vv3-5), and the closing contrast between empty longing and God-given joy and peace (vv6-8).

units.label: Answer me, and hear the doubters
units.note: how long will you love vanity

verses.text: Answer me when I call, O God of my righteousness! You have relieved my distress; show me grace and hear my prayer.
How long, O men, will my honor be maligned? How long will you love vanity and seek after lies? {{selah|Selah}}

units.label: Be still before the LORD
units.note: the LORD has set apart the godly

verses.text: Know that the LORD has set apart the godly for Himself; the LORD hears when I call to Him.
Be angry, yet do not sin; on your bed, search your heart and be still. {{selah|Selah}}
Offer the sacrifices of the righteous and trust in the LORD.

units.label: Joy, peace, and sleep
units.note: You alone make me dwell in safety

verses.text: Many ask, "Who can show us the good?" Shine the light of Your face upon us, O LORD.
You have filled my heart with more joy than when grain and new wine abound.
I will lie down and sleep in peace, for You alone, O LORD, make me dwell in safety.

selah.gloss: A pause or lifting marker, likely musical or liturgical, inviting the singer to let a truth settle before moving on. Here it falls after the rebuke of the honor-seeking crowd and after the call to search the heart in silence.

basic.context: Psalm 4 is often read as an evening prayer paired with Psalm 3's morning cry. David asks God to answer him as He has before, confronts those who chase empty honor and lies, calls everyone to search their hearts quietly rather than sin in anger, and closes with joy and peace sufficient to sleep safely.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm mixes address to God with direct address to people, a reminder that psalms are not only private prayers but also public instruction; the call to be angry yet not sin anticipates and is later quoted by Paul, Ephesians 4:26, showing the Psalms shaping New Testament ethics directly.
basic.originalLanguages: Twice marked by selah, the psalm pauses first after rebuking those who love vanity and lies, then after urging silence and self-examination on one's bed. The peace of verse 8 uses the rich Hebrew shalom, wholeness, not merely absence of conflict.
basic.typology: David's confidence that God alone makes him dwell in safety, sufficient for untroubled sleep even amid slander, anticipates Christ, who slept in the storm, Mark 4:38, and who is Himself the believer's peace and safety.
basic.christ: Jesus is the ultimate answer to 'who can show us the good'; He is the light of God's face shone upon His people, and in Him believers find a joy greater than any harvest and a peace the world cannot give, John 14:27.
basic.god: God is the one who has already relieved distress before, and is asked to do so again, gracious and near enough to fill the heart with joy exceeding any material abundance, and steady enough to make His people sleep in safety.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 4:26 quotes verse 4 directly, be angry and do not sin. Numbers 6:25 uses the same language of God's face shining. Mark 4:38-39 shows Jesus asleep in peace amid a storm His disciples feared would kill them.
basic.oneStory: Psalm 4 teaches that real distress, real slander, and real anger can be brought honestly to God and answered with a peace deeper than circumstances, a peace fully embodied in Jesus, who is Himself the good that every searching heart asks God to show.

context.literary: An evening lament, traditionally paired with Psalm 3's morning cry, moving from petition, through direct address to a doubting crowd, to a closing declaration of God-given joy and peace sufficient for sleep.
context.historical: The superscription, for the choirmaster with stringed instruments, a Psalm of David, situates this among psalms composed for temple worship, addressed both to God in prayer and to a wider audience being instructed in the same breath.
context.canonical: One of several psalms whose closing image of peaceful sleep under God's care, alongside Psalm 3:5 and Psalm 127:2, becomes a recurring biblical picture of trust so complete it rests even under pressure.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model prayer that moves from urgent petition, through honest confrontation of those who slander and chase empty things, to settled trust that produces real peace, teaching worshippers both how to pray and how to examine their own hearts.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific circumstance of being maligned; it prescribes for every believer the practice of searching one's heart in stillness before God rather than sinning in anger, and trusting God's face to shine rather than chasing empty vanity.

debates.question: Who are the 'many' asking 'who can show us the good' in verse 6, and what does this reveal about the psalm's audience?

views.label: Discouraged fellow worshippers
views.case: The phrase reads as a genuine, weary question from people within Israel who cannot see where blessing will come from, to whom David responds by pointing to God's face rather than material abundance.

views.label: The same doubters rebuked in verse 2
views.case: Read this way, the same crowd who loved vanity and lies in verse 2 now voices the emptiness of that pursuit, unknowingly proving David's point that only God, not their vain pursuits, can show real good.

debates.bearing: Either reading reinforces the same pastoral point: real good and lasting joy come only from God's presence, not from chasing status, wealth, or lies.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm's two selahs mark its turns: first after rebuking those who love vanity, hevel, empty breath, and lies, then after the call to search one's heart in stillness on one's bed. The closing peace, shalom, describes wholeness and safety, not merely an absence of danger, a peace David says comes from the LORD alone.

typology.type: Sleep in peace under threat, Psalm 4:8
typology.fulfillment: Christ asleep in the storm, Mark 4:38, and the peace He gives His people that the world cannot give, John 14:27.

study.christ: David asks, who can show us the good, and shine the light of Your face upon us; Jesus is God's face shone fully upon humanity, the good every searching heart longs for. Just as David's peace let him sleep safely amid slander, Jesus slept peacefully in a storm His disciples feared would kill them, and He gives that same peace to His people now, a joy exceeding any harvest and a peace the world cannot manufacture or take away.
study.god: God is the one already proven gracious, who has relieved distress before and is asked to do so again; He sets apart the godly for Himself, hears when called upon, and fills the heart with a joy that outweighs any material abundance, making safety possible even under slander and threat.

crossReferences.note: Paul directly quotes Psalm 4:4, be angry and do not sin, applying it to Christian conduct.
The priestly blessing's language of God's face shining is echoed in the psalm's plea, shine the light of Your face upon us.
Jesus asleep in the storm embodies the same peace David describes, trust so complete it produces literal rest under threat.

study.oneStory: Psalm 4 teaches that honest prayer, honest confrontation of empty pursuits, and quiet self-examination lead to a peace deeper than circumstances allow, a peace David could only taste but that Jesus Christ, the light of God's face and the believer's true good, gives fully to all who trust Him.

sermon.title: Now I Lay Me Down to Sleep
sermon.why: Begg preaches through Psalm 4:1-8, showing that the cry of the heart in its resting moments must be for the vindication of God's glory, and how a softened heart engages the world with compassion and renewed prayer.

quiz.q: What does David ask God to do at the start of Psalm 4?

options.text: Answer him when he calls, show him grace, and hear his prayer, as God has relieved his distress before.
options.note: This opening plea rests on God's past faithfulness as grounds for present confidence.

options.text: Punish his enemies immediately without any prayer for himself.
options.note: The psalm opens with personal petition to God, not primarily a demand for punishment on others.

options.text: Explain why he is suffering.
options.note: David does not ask for an explanation of suffering; he asks God to answer, show grace, and hear him.

options.text: Nothing; the psalm opens with praise only.
options.note: Verse 1 is a direct, urgent petition, not opening praise.

quiz.q: What does David tell the honor-seeking crowd to do in verse 4?

options.text: Be angry, yet not sin, and search their hearts quietly on their beds.
options.note: This instruction is later quoted directly by Paul in Ephesians 4:26.

options.text: Take revenge on those who maligned his honor.
options.note: The psalm calls for self-examination and stillness, not revenge.

options.text: Ignore their anger entirely and suppress all feeling.
options.note: The verse permits anger but calls it toward self-examination rather than sin, not suppression of all feeling.

options.text: Continue chasing vanity and lies without concern.
options.note: This is the opposite of the psalm's instruction, which rebukes exactly this pursuit.

quiz.q: How does David describe the joy God has given him in verse 7?

options.text: Greater than the joy of an abundant harvest of grain and new wine.
options.note: This comparison shows God-given joy exceeding even the greatest material abundance.

options.text: Equal to a modest financial gain.
options.note: The comparison David draws is with abundant harvest joy, a much larger measure than a modest gain.

options.text: Dependent entirely on his outward circumstances improving first.
options.note: The joy described comes from God directly, not from circumstances improving.

options.text: Nonexistent; the psalm ends in despair.
options.note: Verse 7 explicitly describes real, God-given joy, not despair.

quiz.q: What allows David to lie down and sleep in peace in verse 8?

options.text: The LORD alone makes him dwell in safety.
options.note: The verse credits safety and peace entirely to God, not to circumstances or self-reliance.

options.text: His enemies have surrendered.
options.note: The psalm does not describe an external resolution of conflict; the peace is internal trust in God.

options.text: He has secured a strong military guard.
options.note: No military provision is mentioned; the safety described is entirely attributed to the LORD.

options.text: He has forgotten his troubles completely.
options.note: The peace is trust in God amid ongoing distress, not forgetfulness of the trouble.

quiz.q: How does this psalm connect to Jesus?

options.text: Jesus is the light of God's face and the believer's true good, and He slept peacefully in a storm, embodying the same trust that let David sleep amid slander.
options.note: Mark 4:38 shows Jesus asleep in a storm, and John 14:27 shows Him as the source of a peace the world cannot give.

options.text: The psalm has no connection to Jesus or the New Testament.
options.note: Ephesians 4:26 directly quotes this psalm, and its themes of peace and God's face connect clearly to Christ.

options.text: It only describes an Old Testament ritual with no lasting significance.
options.note: The psalm's themes of peace, joy, and God's face carry forward meaningfully into the New Testament's picture of Christ.

options.text: It predicts a specific future event in Jesus' ministry.
options.note: The connection is thematic and typological, not a specific predictive prophecy of an event.

questions: David asks God to answer him again because God has relieved his distress before. What past faithfulness of God can you look back on to fuel your confidence in prayer right now?

David tells the angry crowd to search their hearts quietly on their beds rather than act rashly. When you are angry, what would it look like for you to pause and examine your heart before God rather than react immediately?
David finds a joy greater than any harvest, not tied to his circumstances improving. Where are you currently looking for joy in circumstances rather than in God Himself?

The psalm ends with sleep in peace and safety that comes from the LORD alone, not from David's own security measures. What keeps you awake at night, and what would it mean to entrust that specific worry to God before you sleep?
Jesus slept peacefully in the middle of a storm His disciples feared would kill them. How does His example, and His gift of peace, shape the way you respond to your own storms this week?

translationNotes.note: O God of my righteousness can also be rendered God who vindicates me or God of my right, pointing to God as the one who upholds David's just cause rather than describing David's personal moral righteousness.
Be angry, yet do not sin is quoted directly by Paul in Ephesians 4:26, showing this psalm shaping New Testament ethical instruction almost word for word.
In peace uses the Hebrew shalom, a rich word for wholeness and completeness, not merely the absence of conflict, describing the depth of safety David attributes entirely to the LORD.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 4:1-8. No em or en dashes appear in this psalm's BSB text, and none are used anywhere in this file. Sermon link verified this session by fetching the Truth For Life sermon page for Psalm 4.
